questions.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for logical addressing and routing?
In the TCP/IP model, which layer combines the OSI Physical and Data Link layers?
What protocol resolves an IP address to a MAC address on a local network?
What is the correct PDU order from application down to physical?
What routing rule picks the most specific matching prefix?
What is the main purpose of VLANs?
questions.a: To logically segment a Layer 2 network

questions.q: How does traceroute discover the path to a destination?
questions.a: It sends packets with increasing TTL values and learns each hop from ICMP time exceeded replies.

questions.q: What is the difference between BGP and OSPF?
questions.a: BGP is used between autonomous systems; OSPF is an interior gateway protocol used within one AS.

questions.q: Why do routers care about IP prefixes rather than host names?
questions.a: Routing happens on IP addresses, and prefixes let routers forward traffic efficiently without resolving application names.
Network diagnostics and error reporting, such as unreachable or time exceeded messages.

questions.q: Why does fragmentation exist in IPv4?
questions.a: To let packets cross links with smaller MTUs, although modern networks try to avoid it.

questions.q: What is port multiplexing on the transport layer?
questions.a: Using port numbers so one host can run many networked applications at once.

questions.q: Why are the network and transport layers separated?
questions.a: So routers can forward packets without understanding application state, while transport handles end-to-end delivery semantics.

questions.q: When would you choose UDP over TCP?
questions.a: When low latency or app-level control matters more than built-in reliability, such as DNS, voice, or gaming.

questions.q: Why does NAT exist in most enterprise and home networks?
questions.a: It conserves public IPv4 space and hides internal addressing behind a shared public IP.

questions.q: Why can a switch make forwarding decisions faster than a router at Layer 3?
questions.a: A switch forwards on learned MAC addresses in a local broadcast domain, while a router must examine IP routing state.

questions.q: Why is BGP often described as policy-based rather than shortest-path routing?
questions.a: Because AS operators choose routes based on business policy, not just hop count or latency.

questions.q: Explain why MTU mismatches can hurt performance.
questions.a: Packets may need fragmentation or be dropped, which adds overhead and can trigger retransmissions or path MTU discovery issues.
